design_philosophy.core_loop: Select club and course positioning from bird's-eye view, execute swing timing with power meter and shot type determination from third-person perspective, adjust for environmental factors (wind, ball spin, lie) to minimize strokes per hole
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Precision timing under variable conditions—player must master two-stage swing mechanic (power meter stop, then shot type execution) while accounting for course-specific hazards and physics
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Compete as Mario or Luigi in international golf tournaments across multiple courses, with caddie guidance (Peach/Daisy) providing strategic tips
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Dual-perspective camera system (bird's-eye planning + third-person execution) enforces deliberate shot composition; wind and spin mechanics reward mastery over luck; caddie commentary suggests narrative framing rare for early sports games

difficulty.checkpointing: Stroke-by-stroke progression within hole; hole-to-hole progression within course; tournament structure implies round-based checkpoints
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Regional difficulty scaling evidenced: Japanese version (Mario Open Golf) features 90 holes across 5 courses with higher difficulty; international release reduced to 54 holes across 3 courses with simplified hole layouts to decrease challenge. Players face course unlock progression in Japanese version.

progression.upgrade_system: Club selection system—players cycle between golf clubs with varying properties; bird's-eye view allows pre-swing club changes and aiming adjustments
progression.unlock_mechanism: Course progression; Japanese version requires unlocking courses sequentially (Japan Course available at start); international version presents reduced course set
replayability_hooks: Multiplayer mode (Mario vs Luigi with respective caddies)
Multiple international courses with distinct layouts
Stroke play and match play modes

world_building_techniques: Character roster integration (Mario, Luigi, Peach, Daisy) establishes Mushroom Kingdom sports universe
International course settings (Japan, U.S., Australia implied) suggest global tournament narrative
Caddie role-playing adds light character dynamics to mechanical gameplay

faction_indicators: Mario/Peach team vs Luigi/Daisy team (multiplayer competitive pairing)
danger_signals: Wind mechanics as environmental hazard requiring skill compensation
Ball lie variations affecting shot options
Course complexity scaling (holes modified between Japanese and international versions indicate difficulty tiers)
environmental_storytelling: Course geography (Japan, U.S., Australia) implies international tournament scope
Third-person perspective grounds player in character embodiment rather than abstract sports simulation
